title: African Diaspora Council Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new advisory group within the State Department. The group will have up to twelve members from African American and African immigrant communities. They will give advice to the President about how to build stronger connections between the US government and people of African heritage living in America. The group will focus on making things more fair, strengthening cultural ties, creating educational exchanges, and finding economic opportunities for these communities.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the African Diaspora Council Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Advisory Council on African Diaspora Engagement in the United States
sections.text:
The Secretary of State must create a group called the Advisory Council on African Diaspora Engagement in the United States within the State Department. The African diaspora means people of African heritage living outside of Africa, including African Americans and African immigrants in the United States.

This council will have up to twelve members chosen by the Secretary of State. These members must represent the variety of African diaspora communities, including African American and African immigrant groups. The members should be people who have done important work in government, sports, creative industries, business, schools, social work, and faith-based activities. The Secretary of State cannot choose people based on their political party. The Secretary must talk with the top members of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee and the House Foreign Affairs Committee when picking these representatives.

Each member will serve for two years without getting paid or having their expenses covered. The Secretary of State will pick one member to lead the council as Chair. The Secretary will also choose a senior State Department worker to be the Executive Director of the council.

The council will give advice to the President through the Secretary of State about making stronger connections between the United States Government and African diaspora communities in America. The council will provide information, analysis, and recommendations about several topics. These include ways to make things more fair and create better opportunities for African diaspora communities, including work done by the Domestic Policy Council under Executive Order thirteen thousand nine hundred eighty-five about advancing racial fairness and helping underserved communities through the federal government. They will also look at ways to support the United Nations Permanent Forum on People of African Descent.

The council will recommend programs to strengthen cultural, social, political, and economic connections between African communities, the African diaspora, and the United States, such as the Young African Leaders Initiative. They will work on programs that address challenges and opportunities to help with inclusion, belonging, and public awareness of the diversity, achievements, culture, and history of the African diaspora. The council will suggest programs like the International Visitor Leadership Program to expand educational exchange programs between Africa and the United States.

The council will recommend programs to increase cooperation between public and private organizations and community involvement in improving the economic and social well-being of African diaspora communities. They will suggest programs like Prosper Africa to increase participation of African diaspora members in the United States in trade, investment, economic growth, and development programs related to Africa. The council can also work on other topics the Secretary of State thinks are important.

The Secretary of State will provide money and administrative support for the council as allowed by law and using funds already given to the State Department. The council will meet every three months or more often if needed. After each full meeting, the council will brief the Senate Foreign Relations Committee and the House Foreign Affairs Committee.
